macro_segments.title: Introduction and Context: External Altar Blood Placements and the Mishnah Framework

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and review of Perek 4 overview
Mishnah review: Beit Shammai and Beit Hillel on matanot for external and internal altars
Distinction between external altar (mizbe'ach hachizon) and internal altar (mizbe'ach hapnimi)

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan on the Three Latter Placements of Chatat Blood: Timing, Owner Death, and Outside Offering

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's statement: last three placements cannot be done at night or after owner's death
Liability for placing blood outside (bachutz) parallels the rule for outside offerings

macro_segments.title: Rav Papa's Elaboration: Which Disqualifications Apply to the Three Latter Placements

micro_segments.title: Rav Papa's distinction: some last three placements treated like beginning (ke-tchilatano), some like end (ke-sofan)
List of stringent disqualifications: outside, night, non-priest, vessel, finger, laundering, remainder pouring
List of lenient rulings: meat eaten, no pigul status, no inner sanctuary entry issue

macro_segments.title: The Source for Laundering Requirements: Rav Papa's Proof from Mishnah and Challenges to It

micro_segments.title: Rav Papa sources laundering requirement from mishnah: blood from neck/corner/base does not require laundering, but blood fit for corner does
Scriptural challenge: Leviticus 6:20 uses future tense yize, excluding already-sprinkled blood
Attribution to Rabbi Nechemya: remnant blood (shirei hadam) offered outside incurs liability

macro_segments.title: Baraita on Bloods Requiring the Base and Effective Intent vs. Bloods Poured into the Aqueduct

micro_segments.title: Baraita: bloods requiring base (yesod) require laundering, have effective improper intent, and incur liability if outside
Baraita: bloods poured into aqueduct (amah) do not require laundering, lack effective intent, and are exempt if outside
Digression: note on visiting Machon Hamikdash in Jerusalem for contextual understanding

macro_segments.title: Discrepancy Resolution: The Baraita Applies to Inner Altar Bloods, Not Outer Altar Remnants

micro_segments.title: Gemara challenges: how can Rabbi Nechemya be source if baraita implies both stringent and lenient rules exist together?
Resolution: baraita discusses inner altar (mizbe'ach hapnimi) bloods, not outer altar remainder blood
Logical question: if baraita discusses base-requiring bloods and aqueduct bloods, why not distinguish inner vs outer in text?

macro_segments.title: Ravina's Interpretation: Blood from Corner Literally vs. Blood Fit for Base from Remainder

micro_segments.title: Ravina's answer: corner means literally placed blood; base means blood fit for/intended for base (remainder)
Rav Tachlifa bar Gaza's objection: both could mean blood fit for corner or base
Ravina's counter: if both mean fit-for, it would be redundant; therefore corner is literal, base is fit-for

macro_segments.title: Transition to Inner Altar: Mishnah on Four Placements for Mizbe'ach Hapnimi and Baraita Source

micro_segments.title: Return to second part of mishnah: all four placements on inner altar must be done correctly or as if not done
Rashi introduction: verse ve-asah ka-asher asah is superfluous, suggesting drasha from the repetition

macro_segments.title: Baraita Derivation: Learning from Leviticus 4:20 that Inner Altar Placements Are Indispensable

micro_segments.title: Baraita opens: from ve-asah ka-asher asah, derive that missing one placement on inner altar voids entire service
Source for seven placements on parochet (curtain): indispensable in all cases; need to extend to four on inner altar
Baraita connects to par yom hakippurim (Yom Kippur bull) and chatat offerings for idolatry to establish principle
Inclusion/exclusion argument: include chatat for known transgression (idolatry), exclude festival and Rosh Chodesh offerings
Scriptural analysis: atonement achieved despite lack of semichah and shirayim placement, showing these are not indispensable
Gemara challenges: how do you derive that seven sprinklings are indispensable but semichah and shirayim are not?
Gemara answers: seven sprinklings indispensable in all cases; semichah and shirayim not indispensable in all cases

topical_tags.term: lo sharia / lo mefagla / lo ayla (not affecting meat/pigul/sanctuary entry)
semichah / smichat yadayim (laying on of hands)
